mentions.raw_name: Davos Seaworth (the onion knight, the dead man, Lord Seaworth, Hand)
Wex (mute ironborn boy, Theon's former squire)
Ramsay Snow / Ramsay Bolton (the Bastard of Bolton)
Ned Stark's younger daughter (named as such by Glover)
Ned Stark's two younger sons (believed dead, actually alive)
Prince Aemon the Dragonknight (referenced as exemplar)
Manderly's fool (bed infiltrated by Frey handmaid)
Symond Frey's wife (her handmaid spies for Symond)
New Castle (upper castle in White Harbor)
Seal Gate (city gate where heads are displayed)
Castle Stair (connecting lower and upper White Harbor)
House Greystark (historical — offshoot of House Stark, held Wolf's Den for five centuries)
House Flint (historical — held Wolf's Den for a century)
House Locke (historical — held Wolf's Den for almost two centuries)
Manderly's household/retinue (bannermen, petty lords, hundred landed knights)
The Freys in White Harbor (Ser Jared, Rhaegar, Symond — functioning as a faction/surveillance unit)
Slavers from the Stepstones (historical — captured the Wolf's Den)
Reavers from the Three Sisters (historical — took the Wolf's Den)
Old Gods (Ser Bartimus's faith; heart trees; blood sacrifice — entrails hung in branches; "Your Seven don't know winter")
The Seven / Faith of the Seven (contrasted by Ser Bartimus; The Seven-Pointed Star given to Davos)
Northmen / the North (distinct customs, Old Gods worship, blood sacrifice tradition, "the north remembers")
Southrons (Ser Bartimus's term for Davos and others from the south)
First Men (implied through Old Gods worship and northern customs)
The Whore (Garth's cold black iron torture rod)
The Seven-Pointed Star (holy book, given to Davos for reading)
Davos's farewell letters (to wife and three surviving sons)
Sheepskin map of the north (faded colors, on wall of meeting room)
Glover's dagger (given to Wex, thrown at map)
Silver brooch shaped like a mailed fist (Glover's cloak clasp — House Glover sigil)
Golden trident pin (Manderly's mantle clasp — House Manderly sigil)
Davos's fingerbones (lost; formerly worn at his throat as luck charm)
Iron fetters (four sets, dangling from cell ceiling)
Manderly's warships (fleet built secretly for over a year)
Black Bessa (Davos's ship, referenced for size comparison)
Valyrian (Salladhor Saan's ship, referenced for size comparison)
Palfreys (horses Manderly plans to give the Freys as guest gifts)
Onion (shoved in the dead man's mouth on the spike)
The Seven-Pointed Star (holy text of the Faith of the Seven; Davos reads it in his cell)

mentions.raw_name: Warging / skinchanging (Freys accuse Robb Stark of being a warg who slew Wendel at the Red Wedding)
Heart tree faces (carved face on Wolf's Den weirwood described as "fat and angry"; Wex drew a tree with a face to indicate how he escaped)
Blood sacrifice to heart trees (historical — slavers' entrails hung in branches as offering to the old gods)
War of the Five Kings (ongoing context for the entire chapter)
Red Wedding (Wendel murdered there; Manderly's fury; Frey lies about wargs)
Battle of the Trident (Ser Bartimus saved Manderly's life there)
Battle of the Blackwater (Davos's sons died there; "the day the river burned below King's Landing")
Fall of Winterfell (Theon's capture, Ramsay's destruction — Wex's testimony)
Siege/battle at Winterfell by Ser Rodrik Cassel (Ramsay murdered Rodrik during parley)
Fall of Moat Cailin (recent; Bolton returned north through it)
Stannis's campaign to retake Deepwood Motte (successful)
Wars between Winterfell and the Vale (historical — Osgood Arryn besieged Wolf's Den)
Greystark rebellion (historical — joined with the Dreadfort against Winterfell)
Destruction of House Reyne (referenced by Tywin's threat — the Reynes of Castamere)
King's Hand / Hand of the King (Davos's title under Stannis)
King in the North (historical — Jon Stark, Edrick Snowbeard; recent — Robb Stark)
Warden of the North (Roose Bolton, appointed by Lannisters)
Squire (Wex was Theon's squire; Devan is Stannis's squire)
Knight (Ser Bartimus, Ser Rodrik Cassel, Ser Jared Frey)
Cannibalism (implied at the destination where Davos must go — "men were known to break their fast on human flesh")
Mummer's farce (Manderly's term for his deception of the Freys)
Guest right (central thematic concern — Red Wedding violation, guest gifts, bread and salt)
